planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost risk has passed; this classic Italian heirloom is better adapted to warm climates than many northern types, making it an excellent choice for zone 8–10 spring plantings.

directions: Start seed at 70–80°F 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant deeply after all frost risk has passed; Costoluto Genovese is a centuries-old Italian heirloom widely grown around Genoa and throughout the Mediterranean, recognizable by its distinctive deeply ribbed, lobed, oblate-flattened fruits with rugged, almost corrugated red skin and intensely flavored, somewhat acidic, meaty flesh that makes it arguably the finest sauce and paste tomato in the heirloom world. The indeterminate vines are vigorous and productive; fruits weigh 6–10 ounces and are best used cooked, as the complex, tart flavor concentrates beautifully in sauces and soups. Stake or cage firmly and harvest when fully red and slightly soft; in warm climates this variety often produces two flushes — early spring and again in fall.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; one of the most reliable large yellow tomatoes available, with good disease resistance and consistent fruit set even in warm, humid conditions — well-suited to zone 8–10 spring planting.

directions: Start seed indoors at 70–80°F 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost risk has passed, burying the stem deeply and providing a sturdy cage or stake from the outset. Lemon Boy is one of the most widely grown large yellow tomatoes, an F1 hybrid introduced in 1984 that remains a top performer for home gardeners seeking a reliable, large, lemon-yellow salad tomato; it produces 6–8 ounce round fruits with smooth, bright canary-yellow skin and mild, sweet, very low-acid flesh that is ideal for slicing fresh, for guests who find red tomatoes too acidic, and for adding visual contrast in salads and on plates. Disease resistance to verticillium, fusarium, and nematodes makes it particularly valuable in humid southern gardens. Water consistently and stake firmly as the indeterminate vines are tall and productive.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; a large bicolor beefsteak that needs staking; the striking yellow and red coloring of the flesh and exterior make it a showstopper at market and on the table.

directions: Sow seed at 70–80°F 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant to rich, deeply prepared soil after all frost risk has passed, burying the stem and staking firmly to support the large fruits. Virginia Sweet is a stunning American heirloom bicolor beefsteak tomato with large, 1–2 pound fruits that are golden-yellow with dramatic red streaking on the skin and brilliant pink-red and golden-yellow marbled flesh that is as beautiful to look at as it is flavorful — sweet, rich, and low-acid. It is related to other bicolor heirlooms like Big Rainbow and striped varieties that are prized both for taste and visual appeal; the swirling interior color pattern is most pronounced in cool weather. Harvest when the fruit softens slightly and the yellow shoulder transitions fully from green.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; a Russian black tomato from the city of Tula, south of Moscow; this and similar Russian black heirlooms have become staples of the dark tomato connoisseur community for their striking color and complex flavor.

directions: Start seeds indoors at 70–80°F 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant into rich, well-drained soil in full sun after all frost danger has passed, planting deeply and staking firmly. Black From Tula is a Russian heirloom beefsteak tomato from the Tula region south of Moscow, producing large 8–16 ounce fruits with very dark brownish-purple skin and deep reddish-brown flesh similar to Black Krim but with slightly more robust structure and a complex, smoky, earthy-sweet flavor; it is among the finest of the Russian black-type heirlooms and is particularly well adapted to conditions where hot days are followed by cool nights, which intensifies the dark pigmentation. Harvest when the blossom end yields to gentle pressure and the dark color has fully developed around the equator of the fruit.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; one of the earliest-maturing large heirloom tomatoes, making it excellent for short-season gardens and for getting a jump on the harvest before summer heat sets in at southern latitudes.

directions: Start seed at 70–80°F 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant to well-prepared, deeply amended soil after all frost risk has passed and stake or cage firmly to support the heart-shaped fruits. Anna Russian is a Ukrainian/Russian oxheart-type heirloom with distinctive heart-shaped, pink-red fruits up to 12 ounces that are extremely fleshy, nearly seedless, and have a rich, sweet, mild flavor making them outstanding for eating fresh; it is one of the earlier-maturing large heirloom types at 68–80 days, which makes it especially valuable for growers who want to harvest ahead of the worst summer heat. The indeterminate vines set fruit in loose clusters of 2–3 and continue producing through the season if heat stress does not prevent fruit set; in the South, plant early for spring harvest.
